grammar:
The verb 'heißen' (to be called) literally means 'I am called Anna'. Common pattern for introducing yourself:
• Ich heiße + [Name]
• Mein Name ist + [Name]

Word order in German statements: Subject → Verb → Rest.

Alternative to 'Ich heiße ...'.
Pattern: [Possessive] + Name + ist + [Name].
'Mein' means 'my' (masculine/neuter).

German uses 'sein' (to be) for age, just like English.
Pattern: [Subject] + bin/bist/ist + [number] + Jahre + alt

Note: 'Jahre alt' literally means 'years old' — Germans always include both words.

German nouns have 3 genders, each with its own 'the':
• der (masculine) — der Mann, der Hund
• die (feminine)  — die Frau, die Katze
• das (neuter)    — das Wetter, das Kind

'Wetter' (weather) is neuter, so we use 'das'. Memorize the gender with each new noun — it never changes!

english: We are going to the cinema

grammar:
German uses DATIVE for feelings of cold/warm/well/bad — not the subject case!

• Mir ist kalt  = I am cold  (literally: 'to me is cold')
• Mir ist warm  = I am warm
• Mir ist schlecht = I feel sick

This pattern: [Dativ pronoun] + ist + [adjective]

Don't say 'Ich bin kalt' — that means 'I am a cold person' (personality)!

english: The children play in the garden

grammar:
Perfekt (present perfect) is the most common past tense in spoken German.

Formula: [haben/sein] (conjugated) + [Past Participle at the END]

Example: Ich habe gearbeitet (I worked / have worked)

⚠️ Word order trick: When the sentence starts with a time word like 'Gestern', the verb comes BEFORE the subject:
• Gestern HABE ICH gearbeitet ✓
• Gestern ich habe gearbeitet ✗ (wrong)

This is the V2 rule: the conjugated verb is ALWAYS in position 2.

english: She is not allowed to smoke
Tomorrow we will go for a walk

grammar:
Separable verbs (trennbare Verben) split into two parts in the present tense.

The infinitive 'aufstehen' (to get up) becomes:
• stehe... auf (in main clauses)

The prefix ('auf') jumps to the END of the sentence.

More examples:
• einkaufen → ich kaufe ... ein
• anrufen → ich rufe ... an
• mitkommen → ich komme ... mit

In subordinate clauses (with weil/dass), the verb stays together at the end:
• ..., weil ich früh aufstehe.

I look forward to the weekend
We meet at the train station
We had a lot of fun
He wanted to eat ice cream
She has lived here for three years
We got to know each other
I am going to the doctor
We would like to order coffee
I have been learning German for two years
The car is in the garage
grammar:
Wechselpräpositionen — the two-way prepositions:

These prepositions take EITHER Dativ or Akkusativ:
• in, auf, an, unter, über, vor, hinter, neben, zwischen

📍 LOCATION (where is it?) → DATIV
   Das Auto ist IN DER Garage. (in + der → in der)

➡️ DIRECTION (where to?) → AKKUSATIV
   Ich fahre IN DIE Garage. (in + die → in die)

Ask yourself:
• 'Wo?' (where?) → Dativ
• 'Wohin?' (where to?) → Akkusativ

grammar:
Subordinate clauses with 'wenn' (when/if) follow a strict rule:

📌 The verb goes to the END of the subordinate clause.

Structure: Wenn + Subject + ... + Verb,  [main clause]

Then when the main clause follows the comma, the verb comes FIRST in it (V2 rule, where the entire wenn-clause counts as position 1):

• Wenn ich Zeit HABE, LESE ich Bücher.
  └─── subordinate ────┘ └── main ──┘

Other subordinate conjunctions that work the same way:
• weil (because)
• dass (that)
• obwohl (although)
• während (while)

grammar:
PASSIVE VOICE in German (Vorgangspassiv):

Formula: [werden] (conjugated) + ... + [Past Participle at END]

Active:  Viele lesen das Buch.  (Many read the book)
Passive: Das Buch WIRD von vielen GELESEN.  (The book is read by many)

Key points:
• Use 'werden' as auxiliary (NOT 'sein')
• 'von' + Dativ for the agent (who does it)
• Past participle stays at the end

Use cases:
• When the doer is unimportant or unknown
• In formal/scientific writing
• News reports: 'Das Auto wurde gestohlen.' (The car was stolen.)

grammar:
Past Konjunktiv II expresses hypothetical/unreal situations in the past — what 'would have happened' but didn't.

Formula: [hätte/wäre] + ... + [Past Participle]

Use 'wäre' for motion/state verbs (gehen, kommen, sein), 'hätte' for everything else.

Structure:
• Wenn ich [Partizip] HÄTTE, WÄRE ich [Partizip].
  (If I had ___, I would have ___.)

Example breakdown:
• gewusst hätte = had known
• wäre gekommen = would have come

This is one of the trickiest German structures — common in formal writing and polite hypotheticals.

grammar:
KONJUNKTIV I — used in indirect speech (Indirekte Rede) in formal German, especially in news and journalism.

It shows you're REPORTING what someone said, without claiming it's true.

Formation: usually adds -e to the verb stem:
• sagen → er sage
• haben → er habe
• sein → er sei

Example:
Direct:   Er sagt: 'Ich habe es nicht getan.'
Indirect: Er sagt, dass er es nicht getan HABE.

If Konjunktiv I looks like the indicative, switch to Konjunktiv II:
• 'sie haben' (indicative) = 'sie haben' (Konj.I) — ambiguous!
• → use 'sie hätten' (Konj.II) instead.
